TextENGNew: That strange medicine — the one that refuses to stay in memory — was a capsule, I think.
Or perhaps a larger, oval-shaped tablet.
It had such a vicious red to it... ah, then it must have been a capsule.
I can't imagine a tablet that shade of red.
Yes, it must have been a capsule.
...And I feel like it was just a little larger than a normal capsule.
...I think.
I think.
...I can't remember.
Even as hazy as my memory is, if I were to see that medicine again, I know I would remember it as clearly as if it were yesterday...
That medicine takes things from you.
changeReason: Old's "strips away humanity" more closely translates 人間を奪う ("steals the human/person"), while new's "takes things from you" significantly softens and generalizes the meaning.

TextENGNew: ...It takes your heart, takes your memory, takes your emotions, makes your body heavy and dull as lead.
You can't think of anything. Nothing interests you. You don't want to be awake and you don't want to be asleep.
Even the passage of time loses its shape — sometimes you've spent an eternity only to find the second hand has barely moved one tick,
changeReason: Old reduces a detailed description of time distortion (時間の経過すらも曖昧になり, sometimes spending eternity while only a second passes) to just "There's no sense of time," losing the specific imagery.

TextENGNew: and sometimes you watch the hour hand sweep its full circle as if it were nothing.
changeReason: Old includes content from the previous entry ('a second can feel like an eternity') along with the current entry's content about the hour hand — partial contamination from adjacent line rather than wrong meaning.

TextENGNew: ...But whichever it is, you feel nothing about it.
The medicine makes sure of that.
changeReason: Old renders 抱かせない as "I can't feel emotion" (narrator as agent) while the Japanese causative form means "it won't let you feel" — new's "the medicine makes sure of that" correctly attributes agency to the medicine.

TextENGNew: ...It's a medicine that leaves you with nothing to do but lie in your futon staring at the ceiling.
How terrible and painful a medicine it is — only those who have taken it could ever understand.
And the most terrifying thing about it is that while you're taking it, you cannot perceive just how terrifying it is.
...Day after day, adrift in that fog, unable to see that the medicine is destroying me, swallowing it one after another — I would lose myself forever, piece by piece.
changeReason: Old drastically simplifies, omitting the key horror of being unable to recognize the medicine as harmful while in a foggy state — changed emotional register and missing nuance, but core meaning (medication causing loss of self) preserved.

TextENGNew: So when, by some chance or coincidence, the medicine's effect wore off—
I realized I must never take that medicine again.
...Yes.
This isn't medicine.
It's poison — poison designed to kill me slowly.
I'm certain that if I kept taking it, one day I would fall into a state where I could no longer tell whether I was living or dying, and I would never be able to reclaim myself again.
...Eternal days of nothing but lying there, staring at the ceiling without feeling, watching the clock hands go round and round.
If I took the medicine again, I would lose the thoughts I had only just managed to reclaim.
...And I might never find myself again.
...From the day I understood this, I began pretending to take the medicine and throwing it away.
"When the doctor gives you medicine, make sure you take it all the way to the end — even after you feel better."
That's my mother's voice.
Her voice from every time I was given medicine at the hospital when I was little.
The voice she used to talk sense into me when I'd get well before the medicine ran out and stop taking it.
But this medicine is different.
That voice — the one from an age I can no longer even recall — telling me to take every last capsule even of this garish poison, I drive it out of my head.
I clamp my head firmly between my elbows, press down hard enough to make it ache... and I drive out both the conviction that I must keep taking the medicine, and my mother's voice.
Yes.
...As the fog thinned, the true, ugly nature of my gentle mother came back to me.
My mother betrayed the family.
...She betrayed me and my father, who waited at home for her and had three plates of food ready.
She betrayed our family's trust, secretly meeting another man in the shadows, claiming to be busy with work while she made herself part of a different family.
changeReason: Old condenses to "having an affair" while the Japanese details: secretly meeting another man (別の男と密会を重ね), claiming work was busy, and placing herself in a different family — new preserves these specifics.

TextENGNew: That alone was a devastating betrayal — and yet she committed an even greater crime by delivering her absolute rejection to my father, who had devoted himself so tenderly to caring for her.
But there was something I couldn't forgive even more than that.
...She tried to take me with her — to that family I could never belong to.
changeReason: Old adds "new family" and "only me" not yet established in the Japanese; the Japanese 私とは相容れない家族に誘った means "invited me to a family incompatible with me" — new captures the incompatibility element.

TextENGNew: She didn't invite Father. She invited only me.
Father and I are a family.
Family means being together.
She tried to split that family apart.
Not content with her affair alone, she attempted to sever even the bond between me and my father.
Yes, of course — thinking back on it now, it was something truly unforgivable, something maddening, something I could despise forever...
The back of my eyes began to throb, and I could feel the ferocity of my hatred distorting my face.
The hatred spilled over and would not stop, no longer contained to my mother alone — it reached toward the man, her lover.
...Her lover.
Uncle Akihito.
He used to tease me and I wasn't comfortable around him — but he gave me pocket money, and he was a cheerful man who always tried to make me happy.
I loved him like a dear uncle.
But all that kindness was calculated.
Calculated to shatter the bond of the family that was built from me and Father and Mother.
...That's why I should never have called Uncle Akihito a dear uncle...!
Yes, of course.
It keeps coming back to me, becoming clearer and clearer.
I can see who my true enemies are.
The reason Mother had her affair was Uncle Akihito's fault.
Because Mother was, truly, a wonderful mother.
And it was Uncle Akihito who turned her into a terrible one.
So the most guilty one is Uncle Akihito.
When exactly did Uncle Akihito first appear before me?
Going out with Mother, having lunch at some department store — wasn't it then that a man calling himself a colleague of hers sat down with us?
That time.
...Now that I think of it, back at the very first moment—
Didn't I sense something wrong in the way Mother treated Uncle Akihito? A wrongness, a revulsion?
Yes, of course — I really did feel it.
I thought something was strange.
I thought something felt disgusting.
...I should never have doubted that intuition.
...How foolish I was, to think Uncle Akihito was a kind person simply because he gave me the strawberry from his shortcake...!
Father bears no responsibility in any of this.
He never met Uncle Akihito even once.
But I do bear responsibility.
changeReason: Old's "But I have" is an ambiguous fragment (should reference responsibility from the Japanese 私には責任がある = "I bear responsibility"); new makes the meaning explicit.

TextENGNew: ...I met the enemy that was Uncle Akihito many times, and there were countless opportunities when I could have recognized him for what he was.
Meaning that if I had only held a firm will to protect the happiness I had — if I hadn't been swayed by small acts of kindness — perhaps I could have seen that man as my enemy...!
changeReason: Old captures the core idea (strong will could have helped notice something) but omits the specific details about being misled by deceptions and recognizing 'that man' as an enemy — loses narrative specificity.

TextENGNew: I can't forget that day — Father's face.
Gentle, good-natured Father — digging his nails into his knees, weeping in great falling drops. I can't forget that Father.
He asked me, did I know about Uncle Akihito? And I answered yes, I knew.
And Father slapped me.
...It was only after being slapped that I finally understood.
What a foolish thing it had been — to have been so won over by that man.
Yes, of course... it was my fault.
...The small happiness I had wished would go on forever — I shattered it with my own hands.
If I hadn't been so foolish, Father would never have wept that day, and Mother would have stayed a good mother.
If only I hadn't been so foolish...!
My fault, my fault...!!
I can't forgive Mother, I can't forgive Uncle Akihito.
Ah — and what I cannot forgive even more is myself, still calling him 'uncle' as a term of affection even as I curse him with everything I have...!!
It was my fault, everything was my fault!
Because I was so stupid and foolish that it came to this!!
Full of hatred and sorrow, humiliation and rage that wouldn't cool!
If Mother ever came back to this house, I was going to tear her apart with my own fingernails!!
But Mother never came back.
The rage that had nowhere to go turned itself on every trace of her that remained within reach.
I smashed everything she had left behind in the house, one after another.
Pounding, shattering, breaking.
But my rage wouldn't be satisfied with that much.
I turned the whole house upside down and still my rage, with nowhere to go, would not quiet in the least.
Where does rage gather?
It wells up from deep in the chest, travels through the arms, collects in the fingertips, bursts out through the nails.
Where else is there to strike it?
...When that thought came to me, my nails turned naturally on my own body.
Yes — by now, it was my own body that was most accursed, most unclean.
I was the root cause of everything, I was the worst of all.
changeReason: Old understates 私が全ての元凶で私が一番悪い ("I am the root cause of everything, the worst of all") as merely "I am the one to be blamed."

TextENGNew: No, no, no — I'm not the bad one, Mother is the worst, Mother is the most unclean!
Everything about me — body and soul — is unclean!!
I wanted to defile Rena Ryuugu utterly, leave nothing but scraps of flesh!!
changeReason: Old translates 竜宮礼奈を穢し尽くし as "stain myself" instead of naming "Rena Ryuugu" — the Japanese explicitly uses the character's full name, showing self-alienation, which old misses.

TextENGNew: And then one day, I came to know it at last.
That within the blood filling me, within my veins... something unknowable existed.
changeReason: Old invents "maggots" not present in the Japanese (得体のしれない何か = "something unknowable/unidentifiable"); new correctly translates the vague, mysterious nature of what the narrator perceives in her blood.

TextENGNew: Yes... I was filthy from the inside.
And my heart, too, had grown murky and stagnant...!
changeReason: Old's "Come on, rot more" is not present in the Japanese (心さえも濁って、淀んで = "my heart grew murky and stagnant"); new correctly translates the description of emotional degradation.

TextENGNew: So that was why my skin itched all over — itched and itched and itched and itched and itched and itched!
changeReason: Old omits the causal connection (だから、そのせいで = "that's why") and the Japanese's frantic repetition of 痒くて (itchy) that conveys the escalating desperation.

TextENGNew: When I carelessly injured myself in a minor accident... blood welled up from the wound.
changeReason: Old fabricates graphic details ("stabbed my thigh with a razor blade in the bathroom," "reddish-black maggots") not present in the Japanese (ちょっとした不注意で怪我 = "a small careless injury"); new accurately translates the understated original.

TextENGNew: And mingled with that blood... something revolting began to show itself...!
changeReason: Old fabricates "forced myself to open blood vessels with my thumbs" and "maggots" not in the Japanese (気持ちの悪い何か = "something disgusting/foul"); new correctly translates the vague horror.

TextENGNew: Crawling, crawling, crawling, crawling crawling crawling crawling, crawling crawling crawling crawling crawling crawling crawling...!!
This repulsive "thing" wasn't limited to where I was injured.
changeReason: Old fabricates "maggots from my left thigh" and "right thigh" not in the Japanese (不快な『モノ』= "unpleasant Thing"); new correctly translates the abstract, unnamed horror spreading beyond the wound.

TextENGNew: Arms, legs, stomach, face, head — I couldn't stop feeling it seething and crawling all through every part of my body, squirming through me...!!
changeReason: Old lists wrong body parts (calves, chest, neck) vs the Japanese (腕/arms, 足/legs, お腹/stomach, 顔/face, 頭/head) and invents "maggots in neck blood vessels"; new correctly translates the body part list.

TextENGNew: Strangely enough, even as I felt horror and revulsion at this Thing mixed into the deep red blood, a part of me remained calm.
And I thought, with surprising calm, that if the blood still flowing from this wound didn't stop, I would eventually die.
changeReason: Old invents "ripping myself apart" not in the Japanese; the original describes passively observing blood flowing from a wound (この傷からあふれる血液が止まらなかったら) and calmly acknowledging potential death.

TextENGNew: But everything was my responsibility, and I was the worst of all.
Even if I blamed it on Mother, there was nothing left in my heart with which to accuse her.
changeReason: Old mistranslates as "the only way I can disgrace her is with my own body" while the Japanese means "I no longer had the mental/emotional capacity to accuse her" (非難するだけの余裕が心に残っていなくて).

TextENGNew: ...Ah, yes. I see.
The only way to put an end to my rage was to hurt myself.
...I think I was reasoning through it in a remarkably clear-headed way.
So in order to grant myself absolution... I needed a fault that wasn't my own.
Someone else's fault.
Such a thing didn't exist.
No matter how I thought, no matter how I twisted it, hurting myself was the only outlet for my anger.
So as long as no fault other than my own existed, I had no choice but to go on this way — hurting myself, suffering, being in pain.
...The way I must have looked right then, seen from outside — it must have been truly ghastly.
changeReason: Old is an entirely different sentence ("no matter how much I scratch/rip") while the Japanese describes the narrator stepping outside herself to observe how ghastly she must look (客観的に見たら凄惨).

TextENGNew: Even as I kept up a pretense of composure, the sharp pain running all through me could not be deceived.
From all the blood that had spilled — or perhaps that was why — my whole body was cold, frozen deep from within.
changeReason: Old invents "naked in the bathroom" not in the Japanese; the original attributes the cold to blood loss (血が溢れすぎた) and feeling frozen from within (奥から凍るように).

TextENGNew: And yet my whole body throbbed and ached, my heart hammering like a warning bell.
Cold and hot, in pain, in sorrow, in rage.
...I sought, from the bottom of my heart, for something that could grant me forgiveness.
And then, at that moment... it arrived.
I feel as though it arrived amid a sacred light.
Something utterly mysterious, mystical, bearing no sense of reality whatsoever.
It apologized.
I'm sorry, it said — over and over, it apologized.
Why was this Something apologizing to me...?
An apology is something offered when a sin has been confessed.
...This Something I was meeting for the very first time acknowledged the wrong it had done to me.
Which meant... I hadn't done anything wrong.
...The one who was truly at fault was this Something.
Aha... ahahahahahaha...?
The pain from all over my body surged over me.
And with it, the pain in my heart surged too.
...Up until a moment ago, I had been telling myself this pain was a punishment I deserved.
But since I hadn't done anything wrong — since it was truly this Something's fault — I didn't have to accept this pain as my punishment.
...So I didn't have to hurt my own body anymore.
She went on apologizing — on and on, endlessly — to me as I lay crushed at the bottom of self-loathing.
Every time I wanted to blame myself, she apologized, and kept whispering to me that it wasn't my fault.
Oyashiro-sama is here.
She is always beside me.
Pitter-patter, pitter-patter.
And every time I came close to breaking under the weight of my guilt, she apologized and told me — Rena did nothing wrong.
It was all Oyashiro-sama's curse.
...Because we broke the prohibition against leaving Hinamizawa, everything went wrong.
...Now I remember.
When we moved away from Hinamizawa, the old woman next door gave me a charm.
She said that those who leave Hinamizawa will suffer Oyashiro-sama's curse, so take this protective charm with you.
Yes, she did — she really did give it to me.
She gave me something that looked like formal paper, with difficult-looking writing in brush ink.
But I can't recall ever seeing it again after we moved.
It must have gotten lost somewhere in the move.
Then there was nothing for it.
Those who are of Hinamizawa and leave — that is their destined fate: to suffer the curse.
Me and Mother, and perhaps even Uncle Akihito — we had all gone wrong because of Oyashiro-sama's curse.
No one was ever wrong in the first place.
Because it was all Oyashiro-sama's curse.
No one is wrong, no one, no one, no one!
At last I could forgive everything.
The rage that had defiled everything around me and myself, body and soul, and still would not be satisfied — it had finally quieted.
Father.
...I finally understand.
That Mother left us, that both you and I have been made so sad — it was all Oyashiro-sama's curse.
Father, do you remember Oyashiro-sama?
Oyashiro-sama — the guardian deity of Hinamizawa.
...Yes, that's right.
The people of Hinamizawa must not venture out into the defiled secular world — that's how it was.
That's why Mother became 'unclean.'
changeReason: Old translates 汚れて ("became defiled/unclean") as "went insane" — the Japanese uses the specific concept of ritual impurity (汚れ), which is thematically crucial to the curse narrative; new preserves this with "became unclean."

TextENGNew: That's why it's Oyashiro-sama's curse.
If our family had never left Hinamizawa, surely we would still be happy now.
Oyashiro-sama came to me to tell me that.
Now that I think of it, Oyashiro-sama had been trying to tell me all along.
Since back when Mother left, when I had no idea what had caused it all, when I didn't know what to direct my anger toward — she was always following me, trying to tell me.
Following me all that time.
Softly, quietly.
But I pretended not to notice.
...Ahaha, I'm so dense, aren't I.
I couldn't hear Oyashiro-sama's voice until I had cut up my body this much.
If only I had listened more closely to Oyashiro-sama's voice —
...I think she may have been warning me from around the time Uncle Akihito appeared.
Because, because — if we had all gone back to Hinamizawa as a family, Mother never would have had an affair.
We could have lived happily there, the three of us in Hinamizawa.
That's what Oyashiro-sama had been trying to say — yes, that's right — Oyashiro-sama must have been trying to tell us from the moment we left Hinamizawa.
She was always with me.
Always, always, following me wherever I went.
It was foolish of me to dismiss it as imagination, to pretend I hadn't noticed.
...Yes, because I was so foolish it came to this.
...If I had only heard Oyashiro-sama's voice, our family would never have been made unhappy by the curse...!
I was foolish, I was dense — it's all my fault!!
Ah, Oyashiro-sama is apologizing, she keeps apologizing — that it's her own fault things came to this...!!
Yes, of course — so it isn't my fault, is it?
Because this is all Oyashiro-sama's curse, right?
She's apologizing, apologizing — I'm sorry, I'm sorry, I'm sorry!
Oyashiro-sama is apologizing so much!!
So I'm sorry too — I'm sorry, I'm sorry, I'm sorry!!
I'm sorry I couldn't hear your voice — I'm sorry, I'm sorry, I'm sorry!!
We are people of Hinamizawa — we must always be listening for your voice!!
Ah, she's here — Oyashiro-sama is here, she's really here!
Can't you hear her, Father?
Can't you see her?
She's apologizing right there, right now — I'm sorry, I'm sorry!!
I'll apologize too — Father, let's both apologize! Mother doesn't need to apologize — Mother can just die from Oyashiro-sama's curse!!
I hate Mother, I hate her, I hate her, I hate her!!
I hate that Mother's blood flows through my veins!!
changeReason: Old translates お母さんの血が私に流れているのが憎い as 'I hate myself for being your daughter' — captures the self-directed hatred related to the mother, but loses the specific blood imagery that connects to the broader blood/parasite horror theme.

TextENGNew: So all this pain and suffering is Mother's fault — the something in my blood is thrashing around making me itch!!
I'm sorry, I'm sorry!!
I'm sorry, I'm sorry!
No — no! I won't take that medicine!!
That's something that hateful, hateful Mother brought — so I won't have it, I won't take it!!
changeReason: Old adds "to kill me" not present in the Japanese (憎い憎いお母さんが持ってきたもの = "thing that hateful Mother brought"); new accurately preserves the emotional accusation without the fabricated intent.

TextENGNew: I said I won't take it, stop it, stop!!
This is a curse — medicine can't heal it!
The itch in my body can't be cured by medicine — because it's a curse!!
changeReason: Old substitutes "maggots" for the Japanese 痒み ("itch/itchiness"); new correctly translates the symptom as bodily itching, which is the actual Hinamizawa Syndrome symptom.

TextENGNew: I don't want a shot either — stop it, stop!!
It hurts — it hurts, it hurts, it hurts, it hurts...!!!
......The world drains down into grey.
Father's voice, the doctor's voice, clamoring around me — like something happening far away.
Even what happened just a moment ago feels like the distant past.
...And they give me that capsule again, and even the vivid memories of this moment are buried in the depths of forgetting...
......Once again I am lying in my futon, staring at nothing but the ceiling.
The only thing in my field of vision that moves, the only thing that holds my interest, is the clock's hands.
...Tick-tock, tick-tock.
Watching the minute hand sweep around, watching the hour hand trace its slow circle.
changeReason: Old translates 夕暮れ時 ("dusk/twilight") as "night," changing the time of day; new correctly renders it as "at dusk."

TextENGNew: Other things enter my view.
Those too, like the clock hands, repeat the same thing over and over, endlessly.
I'm sorry. I'm sorry.
I'm sorry — it's my fault.
...Oyashiro-sama goes on apologizing endlessly.
Tick-tock. Tick-tock. I'm sorry. I'm sorry.
Tick-tock. Tick-tock. I'm sorry. I'm sorry.
...That alone goes on and on and on, repeating......
The cry of the higurashi, stealing in through the window from outside at dusk, seemed — for some reason — to ring especially loud...
